# #lock Protocol

**How to use this file:** Step-by-step checklist for when Justin says `#lock` or it's time for a checkpoint. Follow the steps in order.

**Do not edit this file without Justin's approval.** Propose changes; don't make them directly.

---

**Before writing anything below, re-read and follow `docs/protocols/ac-writing-protocol.md`.** A #lock produces multiple writes to persistent files in rapid succession — current-state, CHANGELOG, ARCHITECTURE, the BUILD doc, session docs. If you don't have the writing protocol fresh, you'll make exactly the mistakes it exists to prevent — stale edits on shared files, missing truncation canaries, over-compressed context — that corrupt files other instances depend on.

When Justin says `#lock` or it's time for a checkpoint:

1. **`CHANGELOG.md` (per-repo)** — Add brief one-liners for anything built or changed this session, **to the `CHANGELOG.md` of each repo the change touched.** Changelogs are per-repo: the responsible AC owns the entry and routes it to every repo the change actually hit (the **cross-repo rule**); a change that touched `_evryn-meta` also goes in the meta changelog (every AC reads that on load). Log *substantive ships/changes*, never session narration — the full "what belongs / who writes / routing" rule is in each `CHANGELOG.md` header. *(First on purpose: with the changelogs fresh in hand, the current-state refresh below can safely move shipped items *out* — the changelog is the record that makes a cut safe.)*
2. **Deploy log (`docs/deploy-log.md`) — if a production deploy (`railway up`) happened this session, record it.** The deploy ledger is the single source of truth for *what shipped to prod, when, under which version.* Add a new **`v0.2.N`** row (newest-first) with the date, the deployed commit + its **git tag**, and a one-line "what shipped"; and confirm the matching `git tag v0.2.N` is on the deployed commit in `evryn-backend` (push it). The **ledger + an item's STATUS are the authoritative pair** for "did X ship?" — a *"riding round/wave-X"* tag is intent, never proof (this is the exact gap that made the determination-sharing guardrail look shipped when it wasn't). Also bump `docs/current-state.md`'s "Live deploy" line to the new version. **No prod deploy this session → skip this step.** (The version convention — one `v0.2.N` bump per prod `railway up`, no leading zero, git-tagged — lives in `docs/deploy-log.md`'s header.)
3. **`docs/current-state.md`** — Refresh to reflect current reality. **Clean snapshot, not a log:** fold your update *into* the snapshot — don't append a new dated `*Updated:*` stamp on top of the old ones. When something has shipped or been superseded, it moves *out* of here and into `CHANGELOG.md` / `deploy-log.md`.

   **Keep it current — but flag-don't-delete what you can't confirm.** If something looks clearly stale *and* the changelog / deploy-log / runtime confirms it shipped or died, cut it — that's good hygiene, and those records are what make it safe to cut. But if you **don't know** whether an item is still live (it may be another instance's in-flight lane you lack context on), **flag it to Justin — don't delete it.** Removing a peer's still-live state because you didn't recognize it is worse than leaving one stale line.

   **Reconcile the OTHER direction too — did a ship get MISSED?** Beyond cutting stale items, scan the **recent entries in the relevant repos' changelogs** (+ the meta changelog) *and* the **runtime** (git log / deployed state) for anything that shipped but current-state doesn't reflect. **A ship can creep down the changelog unseen while current-state lags** — that's the failure that leaves agents not realizing something's already done (and re-proposing it a month later). If current-state is missing a recent ship, add it. *(current-state is the load-in snapshot; the changelog + runtime are ground truth — keep the snapshot honest against both.)*
4. **Founding Team current-state** — AC is the ambassador for the build (DC/QC/OC don't write here; AC represents self + them). **Append** to the *end* of `evryn-team-workspace/shared/current-state/current-state.md` — never overwrite. Start your entry with your name (AC, AC0, AC1) and a proper timestamp: `**<your instance> — <full ISO timestamp>:**` — e.g. `**AC1 — 2026-06-13T16:52:57-07:00:**` (or just `**AC — …:**` if you're unnumbered). **Avoid self-descriptor additions unless absolutely necessary** — e.g. `AC2 (BUILD doc realignment)`. The team will sometimes need to do this, because there might be two Sorens working at once — but this extra labeling is rarely necessary for an AC, because that's what the numbering convention is for.

    **Keep it SHORT — a few lines.** The appendage has had a tendency to balloon into a build log; it is not one. The audience is the **whole founding team** (Lucas, Emma, Mira, Marlowe, Nathan, Thea, Soren) + Justin, reading at *their* altitude — most never touch the code.

    **The test for every line:** *"Would the non-technical members of the team think or act differently because of this?"* If a line only means something to someone reading the runtime, it belongs in `_evryn-meta/docs/current-state.md` (Soren reads that for build depth) — **not here.**
    - **IN:** what shipped at the *product* level, what's blocked and why the team should care, big movements (go-live progress, a major decision, a constraint everyone holds).
    - **OUT:** file/function names, commit SHAs, deploy IDs, QC finding labels, the build play-by-play. **Naming a runtime file/function or a SHA *as build detail* is the smell — if you're doing that, you're very likely writing the wrong doc.** (The nuance: a *pointer/link to a doc the reader should go open* — e.g. linking the active sprint doc so the team can find it — is the exception, and is often right. The bar is *play-by-play build detail* vs. *a signpost to where the fuller picture lives*.)

    Target: one short paragraph or 2-4 tight bullets.
5. **Doc-currency gate — the docs that describe the running system (hard gate: #lock isn't complete until this is *answered*).** For EACH doc below, answer explicitly: *did this session change what it claims about the system?* For every "yes," either **update it now** or **route it** (name the section + owner). A deliberate *"no change"* is a valid logged answer, but a silent skip is not. Use `docs/protocols/doc-update-routing-protocol.md` for the exact "which change → which section, and what AC edits directly vs. routes to an owner" mapping — so this is a *lookup*, not a memory exercise (it also names the *other* surfaces a change can reach, several of which are their own steps below). **⚠ The architecture/build/sprint docs have been going stale — give this real attention, not a skim.**
    - a. **The active sprint doc** — mark completed steps DONE, add new steps, reshape. (DC/QC don't write here.)
    - b. **The active build doc** — flip phase badges to match what actually shipped; if it carries an "Active session" pointer and that session content is now absorbed into persistent docs, clear the pointer. 
    - c. **The relevant architecture doc** — status badges + prose still match the runtime. Remember **built ≠ merged ≠ deployed** — don't flip a badge to "live" on a mere merge; a config-gated ship reads *"shipped, inert until `<FLAG>`,"* not "live."
    - d. **Learnings to their RIGHT HOME (the persist-learnings standing order — CLAUDE.md).** Did this session *teach* something durable — how to reason about a codebase, a recurring failure mode, a design constraint you only learned by hitting it? Route it by altitude: **architectural** (a property of the system, e.g. "this runtime punishes local reasoning") → the ARCHITECTURE doc; **build-mechanics** → the BUILD doc; **this-sprint** → the sprint/tracker; **a cross-cutting review/build pattern** → `LEARNINGS.md` / `AGENT_PATTERNS.md` (steps 11–12). A handoff is where a learning is first captured, **never** where it lives — if the only place it exists is a session/handoff doc, it hasn't been persisted.

    **Why this gate exists:** drift accumulated here for months (a shipped feature whose "blocker" badge never flipped — nearly triggering duplicate build work; a renamed table the docs never caught up to) until a full-history reconciliation was needed; this gate is the prevention.
6. **Identity files (`evryn-backend/identity/`) — their own review protocol.** If this session produced or merged any change under that path — Evryn's `core.md`, `situations/*`, `activities/*`, `internal-reference/*`, `public-knowledge/*` — the change goes through `evryn-team-workspace/shared/protocols/identity-file-review.md` (branch + PR + reviewer + checklist + `#dev-alerts` ping on merge). AC is the default reviewer. Confirm here that the protocol was followed; Mira is the owner of identity docs, so if you authored or merged identity-file content yourself, make sure either Mira and/or Justin signed off on it.
7. **Hub & spokes** — If anything this session changes company-level truth (Hub) or domain depth (spokes), update them (the Hub is Justin's — make sure he is able to vet in SCM before any commit). Also check: if a spoke was modified, does the Hub's corresponding section still set the right expectation?

   **Company-context module.** If the Hub changed substantively, its **public-safe derivative — the `evryn_knowledge` `company-context` DB row — must be refreshed** (route to Justin/Lucas). Easy to miss because it's a DB row, not a file.
8. **Cross-runtime safety propagation.** *Did this session close a **security or safety-boundary** hole in ANY runtime we operate?* (Not an ordinary bug — an auth gate, an isolation boundary, an approval gate, a dormancy/kill switch, an egress guard.) If **yes:** name the **class** of the hole, and check that class against **every OTHER runtime we operate** — the Evryn product (`evryn-backend`), the team-runtime (`evryn-team-runtime`), the dashboard. We build these in parallel *in different lanes*, and each lane reviews only its own diff — so a hole closed in one silently re-grows in another unless someone with the cross-lane view checks. That someone is the top-level AC at this gate. A deliberate *"no security/safety hole closed this session"* is a valid logged answer; a silent skip is not. And be honest with yourself: if this session *did* close a significant security or safety boundary, consider that you're deep in your *current* context, so you likely lack the information to answer the question of its relevance in other runtimes yourself. Unless you feel very confident that you know how this would affect other runtimes, send an AC subagent to evaluate this in relation to the other runtimes.
9. **Decisions** — If decisions were made this session, determine if they're session level or architectural. If only session level, ensure they're captured in the session or working doc. Session docs are disposable — absorb and archive/delete, so if they are more permanent, ensure they're captured into persistent docs. **If any decisions this session are architectural (change how the system works, not just craft/process choices), write them as ADRs now — decisions captured only in session docs often get lost in the shuffle over time**.
10. **Retire stale session & working docs — but surface before moving.** Scan `docs/sessions/` AND `docs/working/` for docs this session has superseded (an old handoff replaced by a newer one; a lane brief whose work is settled and absorbed). If you find candidates, **list them for Justin and get his explicit OK before moving any to `docs/sessions/historical/`.** Some old docs are still in play — he wants veto power. **Never move a session or working doc silently.**
11. **`LEARNINGS.md`** — Add appropriate cross-project patterns or insights.
12. **`AGENT_PATTERNS.md`** — Add appropriate agent-building learnings.
13. **Linear** — Update the status of any of your tickets that need it. If this session produced work that doesn't belong in a build, project, or session doc, create a ticket per `evryn-team-workspace/shared/protocols/linear-protocol.md`. If you changed something in someone else's domain, send them a ticket so they know.
14. **Bitwarden reminder** — If `.env` was modified, remind Justin: "Hey, we updated .env — remember to re-upload to Bitwarden." (Files in `.gitignore` exist only on the local machine — git won't save them. Most credentials can be regenerated from service dashboards, but it's time-consuming. Prevention beats cure.)
15. **Mailbox check** — Peek at `dc-to-ac.md` and `dc-architecture-notes-for-ac.md` in any repo you touched this session. If there's content you haven't absorbed, absorb it now.
16. **Operator guide** — If anything built or changed this session affects how Justin operates Evryn, update the relevant repo's `docs/operator-guide.md`. DC flags operator-relevant changes in dc-to-ac.md; AC updates the guide.
17. **Database — backups + schema-reference.** Pro takes daily auto-backups (primary). For the archival layer: if the newest `pg_dump` in `evryn-backend/backups/` is more than a week old, take a fresh one — method in `backups/README.md`. **And if a schema migration ran this session** (added / renamed / dropped a table or column, new RPC / trigger / RLS), **re-pull `evryn-backend/docs/schema-reference.md` from Supabase** — it's a generated snapshot, not authored prose, so it drifts silently precisely because nobody owns re-pulling it. (A new `public` table also needs `GRANT … TO service_role` + `ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` — see the routing protocol, change-type 2.)
18. **Auto-memory hygiene** — Check `.claude/projects/*/memory/MEMORY.md`. AC does not use auto-memory (see CLAUDE.md Auto-Memory Hygiene). If anything landed there accidentally, promote it to persistent docs or clear it. The file should contain only the "DO NOT WRITE HERE" notice.
19. **Settings.local.json cleanup** — Check for `.claude/settings.local.json` in the current repo. This file silently accumulates one-off command approvals at runtime. Delete it if it exists — permissions belong in `.claude/settings.json` (in git), not in local overrides. If it contains secrets (API keys, UUIDs that were auto-saved), flag to Justin before deleting.

    **Worktree hygiene.** For each repo you (or the agents you drove) touched, run `git -C <repo> worktree list`. **Reap stale worktrees** — any whose branch is fully merged into the default branch and whose tree is clean: `git worktree remove <path>` + `git branch -d <branch>` (the lowercase `-d` self-guards, refusing an unmerged branch — if it refuses, the branch has unmerged work; stop and surface it). Leave only the canonical tree + any standing per-agent worktree. The *per-loop* reap (remove right after a merge) lives in `_evryn-meta/CLAUDE.md` (Worktree & Branch Discipline); this #lock step is the periodic safety-net for leftovers that slipped through.
20. **Fragment sweep — a *careful, thorough* pass, not a "quick" one.** The temptation at the end of a long session is to skim this as a formality — **resist that.** The category-based steps above catch what you *remember* happened; this pass exists to catch what you *forgot you forgot*, and you only catch that by actually re-reading the whole conversation, beat by beat, with attention. A rushed fragment sweep is the same as no fragment sweep — the fragments it's meant to catch are exactly the ones a skim misses. **Budget real time for it.** Scroll back through the full conversation and look for: intentions that got derailed, decisions that only live in chat, action items acknowledged but never recorded, things Justin asked for that got handled conversationally but never persisted. Resolve or route anything you find.
21. **Pause for Justin — hard stop. Do not commit yet.** Wait for Justin's clear and present explicit command to "push" or "commit" before this specific commit or push.

    **The test:** *"Did Justin's **most recent message** contain a specific **command** to commit or push?"* If no, summarize what's ready and pause. Workflow instructions ("ping me when you're done," "do the lock," "finish this up," "handle it") describe the work, not the commits — they do not authorize commits. Each commit needs its own affirmative opt-in in the immediately preceding turn; prior authorizations do not stack.

    Failure to do this has been shown in the past to **break** important things.
22. **Commit and push** — Get everything to remote immediately.

---

## Transitional Items (remove after SDK migration)

- **`docs/agent-notes-archive/alex-notes.md`** (in `evryn-team-agents`) — For now, update with anything relevant to Alex's working context. This content will be integrated into the new agent structure during SDK migration.
- **`evryn-team-agents/docs/DECISIONS.md`** — Old monolithic file from LangGraph era. Being replaced by ADR format (`docs/decisions/NNN-title.md`). New decisions go in ADRs or session docs until the old file is reviewed for still-living decisions and archived.
